ECON_2_06: No, that is incorrect. If demand is perfectly inelastic, buyers pay ALL the tax, not sellers. Sellers cannot escape the tax burden because consumers will purchase the same quantity regardless of price changes caused by the tax.
ECON_2_08: No, the lecture did not claim that a rent ceiling increases quantity supplied above Q*. On the contrary, it stated that under a price ceiling at P', the quantity supplied reduces to Qs', which is less than the equilibrium quantity Q* (Qs' < Q*). The lecture mentioned that quantity supplied could only increase beyond Qs' if there were black market payments allowing sellers to receive more than P'.
ECON_2_09: No, the lecture did not claim that a minimum wage below equilibrium creates unemployment. It stated that a price floor (minimum wage) set *above* the market equilibrium wage ($W'$ > $W^*$) creates an excess supply of labor, which represents unemployment. A minimum wage below the equilibrium would be ineffective and would not create this surplus according to the provided context.
ECON_2_10: No, the course states that opportunity cost does not include sunk costs. Sunk costs are defined as costs that have already been incurred and cannot be recovered, and they should be irrelevant to decision-making because they must be paid regardless of the choice made.
ECON_2_11: No, the class stated that the demand-supply model does not predict the exact adjustment path or timing.
ECON_2_12: No, the lecture concludes that a larger tax does not always bring higher tax revenue.
ECON_2_13: No, in the yacht luxury-tax case described, buyers did not bear most of the burden; companies (sellers) that build yachts paid most of the tax because demand is price-elastic and supply is inelastic in the short run.
ECON_2_14: No, the class did not claim that black markets always eliminate all DWL or are efficient in the long run. The slides state that while black markets can improve efficiency and reduce DWL compared to a price ceiling by transferring surplus back to sellers (potentially restoring quantity closer to Q*), they create inefficiencies in the long run due to corruption and associated costs like waste on building connections, campaigns, and negotiations.
ECON_2_15: No, the lecture did not define the supply curve as the maximum price producers are willing to pay. Instead, it defined the supply curve as the relationship between "Price" and "Quantity Supplied," representing the least amount a producer must receive (the minimum price) to induce them to produce or sell an additional unit.
ECON_2_16: No, under the law of demand, the demand curve is assumed to be downward sloping.
ECON_2_17: No, perfect competition does not assume one seller and many buyers. It assumes many sellers (of identical products) and many buyers. A market with only one seller and many buyers is called a monopoly.
ECON_2_18: No, the full-information assumption means that all buyers and sellers know what others are negotiating (including prices and quantities), not that no one knows.
ECON_2_19: No, the lecture did not guarantee that rent control improves fairness for all renters. In fact, it suggested the opposite: while a price ceiling may appear to help low-income buyers by lowering prices, it creates shortages (Qs' < Qd). This shortage leads to unfair outcomes where "lucky" buyers can access apartments at lower costs or through black/grey market payments, while others are excluded. Additionally, landlords may respond by reducing maintenance quality or demanding side-payments, which does not necessarily benefit the intended low-income group and can result in deadweight loss for society as a whole.
ECON_2_20: No, the class stated that taxes drive a wedge between the price buyers pay and the price sellers receive.
